original_abstract:
Radioimmunotherapy (RIT) using monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) is often limited by poor tumor penetration and slow blood clearance, which leads to dose-limiting myelotoxicity. While smaller antibody fragments such as nanobodies (<15 kDa) offer superior pharmacokinetics and deeper tissue penetration, their clinical utility has been hampered by high renal retention. In this study, we investigated the influence of C-terminal amino acid tags on the renal uptake of an anti-HER2 nanobody (2Rs15d) and evaluated its efficacy in targeted radionuclide therapy using Lutetium-177 ($^{177}$Lu).

We designed and produced three nanobody variants: Myc-His-tagged, His-tagged, and untagged. Polarity analysis suggested that C-terminal modifications significantly alter the protein’s charge profile. Dynamic scintigraphy in Wistar rats using $^{111}$In-labeled constructs demonstrated that the untagged nanobody reduced kidney accumulation by 70% compared to the Myc-His-tagged version. Crucially, combining the untagged format with the co-infusion of Gelofusin, a plasma expander, achieved a 90% reduction in renal activity. Similar results were confirmed in HER2-positive tumor-bearing mice, where renal uptake of $^{177}$Lu-labeled 2Rs15d dropped by 95% without compromising tumor targeting.

Dosimetric comparisons revealed that while $^{177}$Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab delivered a higher total dose to the tumor, it also imposed a massive radiation burden on healthy organs—up to 4180-fold higher in the blood and 155-fold higher in the lungs compared to the nanobody. In a therapeutic setting, weekly injections of the untagged $^{177}$Lu-labeled nanobody with Gelofusin resulted in near-complete tumor growth inhibition and significantly improved event-free survival ($P < 0.0001$). Histological evaluation showed no signs of radiation-induced nephrotoxicity. These findings demonstrate that optimizing the C-terminal sequence of nanobodies can effectively circumvent renal toxicity, positioning radiolabeled nanobodies as a highly effective and safe strategy for treating minimal residual and metastatic disease.
humanized_abstract:
Radioimmunotherapy (RIT) with monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) suffers from limited tumour uptake and prolonged blood clearance, resulting in dose limiting myelotoxicity. New antibody formats like smaller fragments like nanobodies (proteins below 15 kDa) having a faster kinetic and deeper penetration within the tumour tissue, are limited for their application in clinic because of high renal retention. Herein, the impact of amino acid tags (tags) at the C-terminal of anti-HER2 nanobody (2Rs15d) on the renal retention and RIT with Lu-177 ($^{177}$Lu) is investigated.

Three nanobody variants were generated and produced as Myc-His-tagged, His-tagged and untagged versions. Based on polarity analysis, the effects of different modifications at the C-terminus on the charge distribution of the protein are shown. In dynamic scintigraphy in Wistar rats with $^{111}$In labeled constructs, the accumulation of the untagged construct in the kidneys was 70% lower compared to the Myc-His-tagged version. Following the injection of the untagged construct, a co-infusion of the plasma expander Gelofusin further reduced renal activity by an additional 10% (90% in total). These data were confirmed in HER2-positive tumor-bearing mice after injection of $^{177}$Lu labeled 2Rs15d. The renal uptake of the lanthanide was 95% lower than the results obtained with the Myc-His-tagged version without any impact on the accumulation in the tumor.

Radiolabeled $^{177}$Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab and a novel $^{177}$Lu-labeled Trastuzumab nanobody for the targeted therapy of HER2 overexpressing tumors In the dosimetric study, the total dose in the tumors was found to be higher in $^{177}$Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab, while the radiation dose to healthy organs, such as blood and lungs, was 4180 and 155 times higher than with nanobody. Following optimization of the C-terminal sequence of the nanobody, weekly injections of the untagged $^{177}$Lu-labeled nanobody with Gelofusin resulted in near complete inhibition of tumor growth and improved event free survival (P < 0.0001). There was no evidence of nephrotoxicity following radiation to the kidneys. Thus, radiolabeled nanobody represents a high potential for the targeted therapy of minimal residual and metastatic disease.

document.input:
Radioimmunotherapy (RIT) using monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) is often limited by poor tumor penetration and slow blood clearance, which leads to dose-limiting myelotoxicity. While smaller antibody fragments such as nanobodies (<15 kDa) offer superior pharmacokinetics and deeper tissue penetration, their clinical utility has been hampered by high renal retention. In this study, we investigated the influence of C-terminal amino acid tags on the renal uptake of an anti-HER2 nanobody (2Rs15d) and evaluated its efficacy in targeted radionuclide therapy using Lutetium-177 ($^{177}$Lu).

We designed and produced three nanobody variants: Myc-His-tagged, His-tagged, and untagged. Polarity analysis suggested that C-terminal modifications significantly alter the protein’s charge profile. Dynamic scintigraphy in Wistar rats using $^{111}$In-labeled constructs demonstrated that the untagged nanobody reduced kidney accumulation by 70% compared to the Myc-His-tagged version. Crucially, combining the untagged format with the co-infusion of Gelofusin, a plasma expander, achieved a 90% reduction in renal activity. Similar results were confirmed in HER2-positive tumor-bearing mice, where renal uptake of $^{177}$Lu-labeled 2Rs15d dropped by 95% without compromising tumor targeting.

Dosimetric comparisons revealed that while $^{177}$Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab delivered a higher total dose to the tumor, it also imposed a massive radiation burden on healthy organs—up to 4180-fold higher in the blood and 155-fold higher in the lungs compared to the nanobody. In a therapeutic setting, weekly injections of the untagged $^{177}$Lu-labeled nanobody with Gelofusin resulted in near-complete tumor growth inhibition and significantly improved event-free survival ($P < 0.0001$). Histological evaluation showed no signs of radiation-induced nephrotoxicity. These findings demonstrate that optimizing the C-terminal sequence of nanobodies can effectively circumvent renal toxicity, positioning radiolabeled nanobodies as a highly effective and safe strategy for treating minimal residual and metastatic disease.
